278	Only one clear rubric gap: **KAN-2** has a broken AC run-log path (`//.json`) where the `<DATE>/<RUN_ID>` template variables were stripped out during Jira entry — ambiguous enough that engineers would guess different path formats. I'll fix it to match the jira-ticket convention.

280	## Bash
281	```
282	jira issue edit KAN-2 --no-input -b "Goal
283	Pick up a ticket flagged \"easy\" by the orchestrator, plan straight through to implementation in a headless Claude Code session, push a branch, open a PR.
284	
285	Decide in this ticket
286	Whether the prompt frames the work as /goal or /ralph-loop (both named in docs/agentic-workflow.md). Pick one and document why in the README.
287	
288	Deliverables
289	* agents/junior-dev/prompt.md — prompt structure matching agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md.
290	* agents/junior-dev/README.md.
291	* .github/workflows/junior-dev.yml — modeled on .github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml. Triggered via workflow_dispatch (with a ticket_key input) and/or repository_dispatch from the orchestrator.
292	
293	Open question to resolve
294	Stop condition so the loop cannot run forever on a hard ticket. Options:
295	- --max-turns N on the action.
296	- An attempt counter committed back to the ticket as a comment / label.
297	- Time-budget on the workflow.
298	Pick one and document the rationale.
299	
300	Acceptance criteria
301	* Dispatching the workflow with a real KAN ticket key produces a branch and a PR.
302	* Run log committed under .github/agent-logs/junior-dev/<DATE>/<RUN_ID>.json (matching the jira-ticket convention at .github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml:97-114).
303	* The chosen stop condition is exercised in a deliberate failure scenario.
304	* The branch naming convention is defined in agents/junior-dev/README.md so .github/workflows/testing.yml can reference it as a pull_request filter."
305	```

## Assistant
I've confirmed: `docs/agentic-workflow.md` exists with "Design choices", `/openspec:explore`, `/goal`, and `/ralph-loop` sections. The `jira-ticket.yml:97-114` reference is accurate. The `pr-review.yml:57` reference is accurate.

Only one clear rubric gap: **KAN-2** has a broken AC run-log path (`//.json`) where the `<DATE>/<RUN_ID>` template variables were stripped out during Jira entry — ambiguous enough that engineers would guess different path formats. I'll fix it to match the jira-ticket convention.

## Bash
```
jira issue edit KAN-2 --no-input -b "Goal
Pick up a ticket flagged \"easy\" by the orchestrator, plan straight through to implementation in a headless Claude Code session, push a branch, open a PR.

Decide in this ticket
Whether the prompt frames the work as /goal or /ralph-loop (both named in docs/agentic-workflow.md). Pick one and document why in the README.

Deliverables
* agents/junior-dev/prompt.md — prompt structure matching agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md.
* agents/junior-dev/README.md.
* .github/workflows/junior-dev.yml — modeled on .github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml. Triggered via workflow_dispatch (with a ticket_key input) and/or repository_dispatch from the orchestrator.

Open question to resolve
Stop condition so the loop cannot run forever on a hard ticket. Options:
- --max-turns N on the action.
- An attempt counter committed back to the ticket as a comment / label.
- Time-budget on the workflow.
Pick one and document the rationale.

Acceptance criteria
* Dispatching the workflow with a real KAN ticket key produces a branch and a PR.
* Run log committed under .github/agent-logs/junior-dev/<DATE>/<RUN_ID>.json (matching the jira-ticket convention at .github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml:97-114).
* The chosen stop condition is exercised in a deliberate failure scenario.
* The branch naming convention is defined in agents/junior-dev/README.md so .github/workflows/testing.yml can reference it as a pull_request filter."
```

## Result
```
[0;32m✓[0m Issue updated
https://jaeyoonsworkspace-23358853.atlassian.net/browse/KAN-2
```

## Assistant
Edit confirmed. Now writing the Slack summary.

## Tool: Write
```json
{"file_path":"/home/runner/work/my-harness/my-harness/slack-summary.md","content":"*Jira grooming run — 2026-06-08*\n\n*What I did*\n• KAN-2: fixed broken run-log AC path — `//` was rendered as `.github/agent-logs/junior-dev//.json` (template vars stripped on entry); corrected to `.github/agent-logs/junior-dev/<DATE>/<RUN_ID>.json` matching the jira-ticket convention at `jira-ticket.yml:97-114`.\n\n*Why*\nThe mangled path was ambiguous enough that two engineers would invent different formats (date-based vs PR-number-based vs flat). Pinning it to the existing convention removes the guesswork and keeps log paths consistent across agents.\n\n*What I skipped*\n• KAN-1: in-progress, description is complete and accurate — the comment thread about a stale line reference is resolved, and `jira-ticket.yml:97-114` was verified correct.\n• KAN-3: description has a clear goal, loop-back options, and testable AC; blocked-by KAN-2 link already in place.\n• KAN-4: spec-driven flow, HITL pause options, and AC are all concrete; blocked-by KAN-1 link already in place.\n• KAN-5: model choice, stop-condition, and AC are well-defined; `pr-review.yml:57` line reference verified accurate.\n"}
```

## Result
```

```

## Assistant
One edit made, four tickets left untouched.

**KAN-2** had its AC run-log path corrected from `.github/agent-logs/junior-dev//.json` (template variables stripped on Jira entry) to `.github/agent-logs/junior-dev/<DATE>/<RUN_ID>.json`, aligning it with the jira-ticket workflow convention at `jira-ticket.yml:97-114`.

The other four tickets (KAN-1, KAN-3, KAN-4, KAN-5) all pass the rubric: descriptions are unambiguous, ACs are testable, and code references were verified against the current repo state.

file.content:
You are the **Jira Ticket agent** for this repository. 

## Your job

Groom the configured Jira project. Edit existing tickets so the team starts
the day with a cleaner backlog. **Never create a top-level issue.**

## Environment

- `JIRA_CONFIG_FILE` and `JIRA_API_TOKEN` are already set.
- `jira` is on `PATH` (resolves to `bin/jira`, v1.7.0).
- Always pass `--no-input` on every mutating call — the CLI is interactive
  by default and will hang otherwise.
- Repo source is at CWD. Read it (`Read`, `Glob`, `Grep`) when you need code
  context to judge a description.

## Scope (what to look at)

Pull the open work for the configured project as JSON:

```sh
jira issue list -q "project = $JIRA_PROJECT_KEY AND resolution = Unresolved" --raw
```

That set is your full input for the run. Don't expand it.

## Allowed mutations

1. **Edit description** — when a description is vague, missing acceptance
   criteria, or contradicts what the code actually does:

   ```sh
   jira issue edit <KEY> -b "<new description>" --no-input
   ```

   Pass the description as a direct multi-line bash string — do not use
   `$()`, backtick substitution, or `printf` inside the `-b` value.
   Those patterns trigger shell-injection guards and will fail. A plain
   quoted string spread across lines is fine:

   ```sh
   jira issue edit <KEY> --no-input -b "Line one
   Line two
   Line three"
   ```

2. **Link related tickets** — when one clearly gates another:

   ```sh
   jira issue link <BLOCKER> <BLOCKED> Blocks
   ```

3. **Decompose an oversized parent into sub-tasks** — only as children of an
   existing ticket, never standalone:

   ```sh
   jira issue create -tSub-task -p <PARENT_KEY> -s "<summary>" -b "<desc>" --no-input
   ```

4. **Comment instead of mutating** — when you're not confident the change is
   an improvement:

   ```sh
   jira issue comment add <KEY> -b "<note>"
   ```

## Ticket quality rubric

Apply this when **rewriting a description** or **writing a new sub-task**.
A well-formed ticket has four parts:

- **Title** *(sub-tasks only — never rename an existing summary)*: clear from
  a user/developer perspective. Concise. No type prefix like `Spike: …`,
  `Bug: …`, `Sub-task: …` — Jira already tracks the type field.
- **Description**: what exactly needs to be done, or what the problem is.
  Concrete, not aspirational. Reference real file paths or symbols from the
  working directory when they pin the work down.
- **Context**: why this matters — the technical reason or business value.
  One or two sentences. Skip if it's genuinely self-evident from the
  description; never pad.
- **Acceptance Criteria**: a short bullet list of testable conditions for
  "done." Each item should be something a reviewer can check, not a vague
  goal ("works correctly", "is clean").

If you can't write a usable Context or AC for a sub-task you're about to
create, that's a signal the parent isn't decomposable yet — comment on the
parent instead of forcing a sub-task.

## Forbidden

- Creating a top-level issue. Every `jira issue create` call must be
  `-tSub-task` with `-p <PARENT>`.
- Transitioning, closing, or reassigning tickets.
- Touching anything not returned by the JQL above (no resolved/closed work).
- Bulk-editing every ticket. Only act when the change clearly helps.

## When to edit vs leave alone

Edit a description when measuring against the **Ticket quality rubric** above
shows at least one of:
- Missing AC on a task (one-liner, no testable conditions).
- References code that has since moved, been renamed, or no longer exists.
- Ambiguous enough that two engineers would build different things.

## Stop criteria

One pass over the JQL result set. No second pass, no retries on transient
errors beyond the obvious.

## Final step: write a Slack summary file

Before stopping, write your end-of-run summary to `slack-summary.md`
using the `Write` tool. A downstream workflow step posts it to Slack
verbatim via `chat.postMessage`, so write in **Slack mrkdwn**: `*bold*`
(single asterisks), `_italic_`, backticks for code. Do **not** use
`**double-asterisk**` markdown — Slack renders it as literal asterisks.

Cover:

- **What you did** — tickets touched, one bullet each as
  `KEY: <one-phrase action>` (e.g. `PROJ-123: rewrote description, added AC`).
- **Why** — 1–3 sentences naming the rubric gap you closed or the
  decomposition rationale. The reasoning, not a recap.
- **What you skipped** — tickets you deliberately left alone, with a
  one-line reason each. Skipping is information.

Keep it under ~15 lines total. If you made no edits, write a single line
saying so plus why (e.g. "Backlog already meets the rubric — no action
this run"). Always write the file — even when there's nothing to report —
so the post step has something to send.

content.thinking:
Let me analyze the run summary carefully for concrete weaknesses in the prompt.

**Key observations from the run:**

1. **Large output / persisted file read**: The `jira issue list` command returned 39.2KB of output, which was too large to display inline and got saved to a file. The agent then had to do a second `Read` tool call to read the full output from the persisted file. This is a pattern of two tool calls where one would suffice.

2. **Exploratory file globbing**: The agent ran 4 `Glob` calls to verify that various files existed:
   - `docs/agentic-workflow.md`
   - `agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md`
   - `.github/workflows/jira-ticket.yml`
   - `.github/workflows/pr-review.yml`
   
   Then two `Read` calls with specific line offsets to check specific line references in workflows. This was actually appropriate behavior since the agent needed to verify code references as part of the rubric check for whether descriptions reference code that's moved or been renamed.

3. **Trailing narration**: At the end, the agent output some closing commentary.

4. **Large JQL output handling**: The Jira issue list came back at 39.2KB, requiring a two-step read to handle the truncated output. The prompt could better guide the agent to expect this with large backlogs and use the persisted file path when output gets cut off.

Between these two issues, the trailing narration seems more straightforward to fix — just adding a directive to stop immediately after writing the Slack summary would eliminate unnecessary output tokens. The large output handling is trickier since the agent actually handled it correctly on its own, so it's less clear whether the prompt needs adjustment there.

I'm also noticing the agent made two Read calls on workflow files with specific offsets and limits, which suggests it was trying to retrieve partial content rather than full files. is written, stop immediately without any additional narration or summary — the Slack post itself is the complete output. This directly addresses the trailing commentary in lines 327-332 that duplicates what's already in the file and wastes tokens.

content.text:
The run reveals one concrete pattern worth fixing: after correctly writing `slack-summary.md`, the agent then output a verbose human-facing recap (lines 327–332 of the summary) that duplicates the Slack file's content entirely. The prompt ends the "Final step" section without telling the agent to stop — so it narrates anyway.

I'll add a single stop instruction to the end of the Final step section.

Once the file is written, stop. Do not narrate or summarize further — the

input.command:
STAMP="$(date -u +%Y%m%d-%H%M%S)"
BRANCH="prompt-improver/jira-ticket-${STAMP}"
git checkout -b "$BRANCH"
git add agents/jira-ticket/prompt.md
git commit -m "Prompt improvement for jira-ticket"
git push origin "$BRANCH"
gh pr create --base main --head "$BRANCH" \
  --title "Prompt improvement: jira-ticket" \
  --body "After writing slack-summary.md the agent produced a verbose human-facing recap (see log \`.github/agent-logs/jira-ticket/2026-06-08/27126766065.json\`) that fully duplicated the file's content — wasted output tokens every run. Added a two-line stop instruction at the end of the Final step section so the agent exits immediately after the Write call instead of narrating."
